title: The Four Phases
body:
Each adventurer has a personal quest that compels them into the lightless depths. Once their quest is fulfilled, they can finally retire and rest. Until then, it is a cycle of descending into the Underworld and returning to the City to restock and rest.

The flow of the game consists of four distinct phases, each with its own style of play and rules.

### The Crawl Phase

During the Crawl Phase, the guild explores an interconnected network of dungeons called the Underworld. During this phase, adventurers test their wits and skills against inhospitable forces in order to fulfill their quests. During the Crawl, players charge their Bonds with their guild-mates. The Crawl is where you’ll spend most of your time.

### The Challenge Phase

A Challenge Phase occurs when you need to focus on the beat-by-beat action. This phase is when choices and consequences bear the hideous, overripe fruit of rotting bodies and terminated possibilities! During a Challenge, play moves from a freeform style to a tactical system. Play is split up into rounds and turns, during which people perform various specific actions.

### The Camp Phase

As the adventurers get worn down by journeys and battles, they’ll pause and use the Camp Phase. The focus of the Camp Phase is interpersonal roleplaying, drama, and connections between members of the guild. During this phase, players burn charged Bonds to rest and recover.

### The City Phase

Each delve is punctuated by the City Phase. The City Phase resolves the entire sequence and gives the guild a chance to spend their hard-earned loot, advance their long-term agendas, and reflect on their progress.

title: The Flow of the Play
body:
Games usually take place at a regularly scheduled time and place. Finding time to play games with other busy people can be challenging. Consistent play is crucial to keeping a game alive. Most sessions have a basic flow:

### Before play

At the start of a game session, a few important pieces of bookkeeping are taken care of:

### Recap

One player—with help from others at the table, and with corrections from the GM as needed—recaps what happened during the last session. This gets everybody back into the game and refreshes their memory about where they were and what they’re doing.

If this player does an exceptionally good job, the GM may give them a point of Resolve. They may keep this Resolve or give it to another player who needs it.

### Returning players

If anyone misses a session during the Crawl Phase, their adventurer is assumed to be about one room away, trailing behind the main guild. They have been slowed down performing a Camp Action. When the player returns, they tell the table what activity they performed, receive the benefit from it, and rejoin the main group. Some actions (e.g., Fellowship, Train) logically require another adventurer to be “absent” with the returning player; only choose activities that make sense for the circumstances.

### During play

A typical game session flows between the different phases. Over the course of many game sessions, play will follow this sequence:

- Decide to pursue one adventurer’s quest.
- Adventure through the Underworld (Crawl Phase).
- Struggle against the strange denizens of the Underworld (Challenge Phase).
- Rest as well as possible in the lightless depths (Camp Phase).
- Return to the City, broken and bleeding (City Phase).
- Spend accumulated wealth to heal, stock up on equipment, and pursue long-term projects in the City.
- Pick up a contract to finance the next crawl.
- Descend back into the Underworld in continued pursuit of the current quest while working on their contract (Crawl Phase).
- Repeat.

### End of play

Play can end after any phase or simply at a specified time. It’s best for the GM to close play when there’s a good stopping point—when one scene has just ended, or just after introducing a new scene (so the players have something to look forward to next week).

The GM can take time at the end of play to get feedback about what the players want to do or where they want to go next. Reminders about when they’ll play next, who might be absent, etc., can be dealt with at this time.

title: Adjudicating the Game: GM Responses
body:
An RPG is a conversation between the GM and the players. The GM describes a scene and the players describe their actions inside the scene. The GM and the players ask questions of each other to further define their shared textual reality.

The GM adjudicates how the adventurers’ actions impact the world. To this end, GMs are armed with the following four stock phrases:

- “Yes, you can do that.”
- “Say more about what you’re doing.”
- “Draw to see if you can do that.”
- “No, you can’t do that.”

These phrases (and their variants) are the four basic responses that a GM has for answering a player when they ask, “Can my character do…?”

### “Yes, you can do that”

The player describes their adventurer’s actions and the GM simply describes what happens. If there’s nothing preventing the adventurer from succeeding, they simply succeed.

- “It doesn’t seem that the door is locked. You open the door slowly and carefully.”
- “Sure, you can climb the tree. There're plenty of handholds.”
- “You know that it will take a few minutes of hard work and it'll make a shit ton of noise, but of course you can use your felling axe to hack down the wooden door.”

Players portray members of the unfortunate caste called “adventurers.” As such, they have the skills and abilities that you’d assume adventurers would have by default.

In practice, this means you ignore all the uninteresting parts of adventuring. You never test fate to see if an adventurer knows how to kindle a bonfire—they’re competent travelers. Don’t worry about what weapons an adventurer is proficient in—they’re competent fighters.

Even difficult tasks can be resolved without a test of fate as long as: 1. there’s no interesting risk; 2. the adventurer has plenty of time; 3. they have the appropriate tools.

If one of these factors is missing, it’s time to move to one of the other responses.

If an adventurer's actions would incur some sort of penalty, the GM should make that clear and give them a choice.

- If an adventurer’s actions would take a long time to complete, the GM should make this clear. If the player elects to spend the time, the GM draws on the Meatgrinder table to represent time passing; similar to the Moving carefully rules. Draw from the major arcana—torches gutter on a result of I-V.
- If an adventurer's actions would create a lot of noise, the GM should give that info. If the player elects to risk the noise, the GM draws on the Meatgrinder table to represent an interested predator investigating the disturbance; similar to the Loud noises rules. The GM draws from the major arcana—a random encounter occurs on a result of XV-XX.

### “Say more about what you’re doing”

Sometimes it’s not exactly clear what actions the players are describing or what their intentions are. In these cases, the GM should ask for clarification.

- “How exactly are you searching for traps?”

“OK, because of the Linguist talent, you definitely speak the myconid language, but you know that their language is expressed entirely in the medium of spore bursts. How do you intend to communicate with the fungoid?”

- “How can you read the text if you don't have any light?”

Sometimes there is a miscommunication in what the GM has described—the GM might be picturing one thing but the players have understood something very differently. Sometimes there's ambiguity in what the player is trying to do. Asking for clarification keeps the shared hallucination of the game consistent by syncing information between the GM and the player.

The GM can also ask clarifying questions about the tactics that the character would be employing. A player’s statement that they want to “Perform a coup de grace on the vampire” might be more successful depending on the precise methods employed—the GM may want to know what weapons are used, what exactly is done, etc.

Once the GM has achieved clarity with “Say more,” they can then shift to one of the other three basic responses.

### “Draw to see if you can do that”

When an action is fraught with danger or its outcome is uncertain, it’s time to test fate. For an overview of tests of fate.

Tests of fate should be relatively rare and relatively tense. Don’t call for tests of fate for inconsequential actions— only when something of importance hangs in the balance.

- “The door is locked. If you have lockpicks, you can test Pentacles to try and pick the lock.”

### “No, you can’t do that”

Sometimes, actions are not possible or will automatically fail based on the logic of the narrative world.

- “No, you can't really try and tame a dangerous wild beast with a single test of fate. Something like that would need to be done by breaking it over the course of several weeks. You can try and capture it, though, if you want...”
- “No, if you don't have any tools, you don't have any way to pick the lock. There's not any, like, bits of wire just lying around your jail cell.”

title: The Major and Minor Arcana
body:
A single **tarot** deck is used in *His Majesty the Worm* to create random results. A tarot deck in its entirety consists of seventy-eight cards of two types: **major arcana** and **minor arcana**. **The GM uses the major arcana cards *except* the Fool.** The major arcarna are numbered and have evocative names like Magician [I] and Wheel of Fortune [X]. Therefore, the GM’s deck has 21 cards, numbered I–XXI.

**Players use the minor arcana cards *plus* the Fool,** which is borrowed from the major arcana deck. There are fifty-six minor arcana cards—reminiscent of a more traditional deck of poker cards—with four suits of fourteen cards. The four suits are Swords, Pentacles, Cups, and Wands. The minor arcana are numbered I–X, and each suit has four face cards.

Each card in the minor arcana has a value. A card’s value equals the number shown on the card. The Fool has the value of 0. Aces have a value of 1. The face cards have the following numeric values:

- Page - 11
- Knight - 12
- Queen - 13
- King - 14

At the beginning of each session, separate the minor and major arcana into the two separate decks and shuffle each. Place the major arcana deck near the GM. Place the minor arcana deck (with the Fool shuffled into it) in a central location where all the players can reach it.

There are two discard piles—one for minor arcana and one for major arcana. As cards are played, place them into the appropriate discard pile. (Occasionally, certain rules will call for you to look at the top card of a discard pile, so it can be important to note this.)

### Whenever the Fool is drawn, both decks are reshuffled at the end of the round.

title: Tests of Fate
body:
When an adventurer’s action within the game's narrative is uncertain, the GM calls for a **test of fate**.

Adventurers have four **attributes**, rated 1-4. Each represents an “approach” towards problem solving, and says a lot about *how* an adventurer achieves their goal.

The GM determines which attribute is most relevant to the test’s outcome. To do this, they ask questions of the player to find out how, exactly, they are attempting the action. The player's description will inform which attribute is being tested.

### Attributes

Swords: Swords represents strength and skill with weapons. Test Swords to bend the bars of your cell or tip over a heavy bookshelf onto an unsuspecting goblin.

Pentacles: Pentacles represents swiftness and slyness. Test Pentacles to sneak past a deathknight or slip the sea witch’s conch into your bag without her noticing.

Cups: Cups represents wisdom and training. Test Cups to activate an ancient clockwork mechanism or coax a wild winter wolf to accept a gift of meat from your hand.

Wands: Wands represents willpower and charisma. Test Wands to convince an art critic that your petrified companion is actually avant-garde art or overcome the dominating gaze of a vampire.

If the player describes being sneaky or quick, the GM will call for a test of Pentacles; if the player describes being mystical or eloquent, the GM will call for a test of Wands—and so forth.

To test fate, the player draws a minor arcana card and adds the associated attribute to the value of the card.

### Success

If the total value is 14+, the result is a success. The GM describes how the adventurer succeeds in their stated goal.

### Failure

If the total value is 13 or less, the result is a failure. The GM describes how the adventurer fails and asks what they want to do next.

### Great success

If the total value is 14+ *and* the player drew the suit being tested on the initial draw (not from pushing fate or another source), the result is a great success. Not only does the adventurer succeed, but they succeed extremely well, going above and beyond what was expected.

The GM should invite the player to embellish how *impressive*, how *effortless*, how *quick*, or how *thorough* their success was.

### Great failure

If the player pushes fate *and* the total value is still 13 or less, the test is a great failure. The GM describes a *new* significant setback, bad situation, or harmful repercussion as a result. See “Pushing Fate.”

title: Pushing Fate
body:
If the result of the test is a failure, the player may opt to **push fate**. Pull an additional card and add its value to the original total. If the total result of the two cards plus the attribute is 14+, the test succeeds as normal. You can never achieve a great success if you push fate. If the total result is 13 or less, the test becomes a great failure.

When you push fate and achieve a great failure, you not only fail to achieve what you set out to do, you make things even worse.

Narratively, a great failure creates new obstacles to overcome or severely hampers the adventurers’ path forward. For example, if a thief achieves a great failure while picking a lock, the sound of the broken lockpick jammed in the door alerts a passing guard, who catches the thief red-handed. Or, if a templar suffers a great failure when attempting to intimidate the ogre, the ogre flies into a rage and sucker-punches the templar in a surprise attack.

Additionally, a great failure inflicts a mechanical penalty on the adventurer who failed. If the situation is a dangerous situation, the GM can inflict a Wound on the adventurer. If the situation isn’t life threatening to begin with, an effect—representing frustration, sorrow, madness, exertion, or some other emotional state—might be more appropriate. For example, the GM might say that until the adventurer’s next encampment they become Exhausted (suffering the Exhaust effect) because they have overexerted themselves.

### Pushing fate with the Fool

The Fool has a value of 0.

- If you pull the Fool initially during a test of fate, you can push fate as normal. This could turn the test from a failure to a success or to a great failure.
- If you pull the Fool when pushing fate, your result automatically becomes a great failure.

When the Fool is drawn, remember to shuffle both the minor and major arcana decks.

title: Favor and Disfavor
body:
Sometimes circumstances can influence a test of fate positively or negatively.

When beneficial factors would aid an adventurer, they are said to have **favor**. Favor grants +3 to the total value in a test of fate.

When negative factors would hinder an adventurer, they have **disfavor**. Disfavor subtracts –3 from the total value in a test of fate.

Favor and disfavor are not cumulative. You do not apply additional adjustments for having multiple helpful or hindering factors. For example, if you have several favorable factors, just note that you have favor and add +3.

Favor and disfavor cancel each other out. If there are both helpful and hindering factors, you simply make the test of fate as normal. Because favor and disfavor are not cumulative, you do not consider how many factors weigh on either side; multiple favorable factors may be canceled out by one disfavor factor.

### Relevant motifs

If one of your motifs is meaningfully related to the test of fate, you gain favor on the test of fate.

### Resolve

Resolve represents an adventurer’s fortitude as they pursue their quests—fortitude that dwindles as you go deeper and deeper into the Underworld. You may elect to spend a point of Resolve *prior* to a test of fate in order to gain favor on that test.

### Aiding others

It is usually assumed that adventurers fill various niches and take on different roles during the Crawl. One is the scout and always does the reconnaissance, another is typically the watchman for the group, and another always forages for herbs. In these cases, the adventurer most suited for the task simply makes the test of fate. Sometimes, however, players will have the opportunity to work together on a test of fate.

If two players can collaborate meaningfully, the acting player gets favor (+3) on the test of fate.

Adding more people to the mix isn’t necessarily helpful. It’s usually best to let the best-suited adventurer take point and have aid provided by the person most interested in helping.

The caveat about aiding another is that both participating adventurers are affected by the consequences of the test. If the test of fate is successful, both succeed. If the test fails, both adventurers fail. However, it’s important to note that the player who takes the lead and actually draws gets to determine whether or not they push fate. In the case of great failures, both adventurers are affected equally.

title: Bound by Fate
body:
Whether you succeed or fail, your results are bound by fate. You cannot retry the same test unless the circumstances significantly change. If you fail to pick a lock, that lock is beyond your adventurer’s abilities. If you fail to climb a wall, that wall is unclimbable.

But maybe the warrior in the company can bash down the door. And maybe the sorcerer has a magical cloak that allows you to fly over the wall. You’ll have to find another way past an obstacle if you fail in the test.

You can also attempt a test again if the circumstances significantly change. Significant change usually means new ideas, new tools, or a new twist on an old situation.

Because *His Majesty the Worm* is a fundamentally collaborative game, fellow guild members are also bound by fate. If one person attempts something and fails, it’s established that it cannot feasibly be done at present. No fair having each guild member try successively until somebody succeeds. However, additional guild members can attempt alternate methods to bypass the obstacle.

It’s important to note the GM is also bound by fate. The GM may not call for multiple frivolous tests to see if an adventurer can accomplish a single task. The more times a successful test is reattempted, the more chances the player has to fail. The GM may only call for repeated tests when the situation meaningfully changes.

title: Group Tests
body:
In the face of discrete challenges, it’s best to have one adventurer be bold and test fate. There are times, however, when the entire guild must test their fates together. For example, the entire guild must sneak into the Tower Gnostic to steal an artifact or must perform the Operetta d’Serpentis perfectly for the master of the Broken Smiles Opera House. In such cases, the GM calls for a group test.

Group tests are accomplished by having two adventurers test fate: the one most qualified and the one least qualified. If there are apparent ties, talk it out to determine who should make the tests.

- If one of the adventurers succeeds in a test of fate, they give **1 hit** to the group test. If they get a great success, they give **2 hits**.
- If one of the adventurers fails in a test of fate, they give **no hits** to the group test. If they suffer a great failure, they subtract **-1 hit**.

The GM totals the amount of hits to determine the success of the group test.

- **2 hits:** Success for the entire group.
- **1 hit:** The group comes short of succeeding and are put in a tight spot.
- **0 hits:** Failure for the entire group.
- **<0 hits:** Disaster for the entire group.

Like other tests of fate, group tests are bound by fate.

title: Effects
body:
Terms that describe consequences that happen to characters or objects things like Blind, Destroy, and Heal are called Effects. Each effect has its own rules and implications, explained in the list below.

### Blind

Blinded creatures cannot see or gain any visual information.

- Blinded creatures make all tests and Challenge Actions with disfavor.
- Blinded creatures can’t make missile attacks at all.
- All harmful actions against Blind creatures have favor.

### Control

Controlled creatures obey the order given to them. As soon as the order is completed, or if it cannot be completed, the effect ends.

- During Challenges, Controlled creatures immediately perform the commanded action. The total value for the Controlled action is equal to the value of the action used to Control it.

### Curse

Curse is a generic term for “bad thing” as determined by the GM. Turned to stone by a medusa? Fused with a party member? Changed into a baby? These are all Curses. The GM will tell you what it means.

### Deafen

Deafened creatures cannot hear or gain any auditory information. Speak Incantation is made with disfavor.

### Destroy

Destroyed objects are broken and unusable.

### Disarm

Disarmed creatures drop one held item of their choice.

- A dropped item requires a Recover action to retrieve during Challenges.
- If an adventurer is carrying neither a melee weapon or shield, all attacks against them gain favor.

### Displace

Displaced creatures are moved to another zone, as determined by the initiating creature or effect.

### Exhaust

Exhausted creatures make all tests and Challenge Actions with disfavor.

### Heal

Healed creatures instantly remove one Wound from a condition.

### Inspire

Inspired creatures must immediately role-play one of the seven Dispositions to the best of their ability, following the basic guidelines. The Dispositions are: anger, distaste, sadness, joy, surprise, trust, fear.

### Grow/Shrink

Grown creatures and objects double their original height. Shrunk creatures and objects half their original height.

Gain favor when this would be advantageous (squeezing into holes, sneaking around) and disfavor when it would be disadvantageous (contests of strength).

### Knockout

Knocked-out creatures are unconscious and incapacitated. They cannot take any actions.

### Notch

When an item suffers an attack that compromises its durability, it is Notched. After suffering a certain number of Notches, the item is Destroyed.

- Fragile items are Destroyed after one Notch.
- Items intended for durability are Destroyed after two Notches.
- Items made of tempered steel are Destroyed after three Notches.
- Notched items can be repaired with a tinker's kit or in the City.

### Root

Rooted creatures cannot move from the spot on which they are standing.

- They cannot Avoid, Dash, Dodge, or Move.
- Anybody engaged with a Rooted creature immediately disengages.

### Shroud

Shrouded creatures are invisible. Shrouded creatures are impossible to see without the aid of magic or special senses.

- Typically, if a Shrouded creature stands still and stays quiet, they cannot be deliberately targeted by creatures who can’t see them.
- However, if the Shrouded creature is moving and interacting with their environment, they can be targeted, though all such attempts are made with disfavor.
- Shrouded creatures make harmful actions with favor against creatures who can’t see them.

### Silence

Silenced creatures cannot make any vocal sounds. As such, they cannot cast spells because they cannot Speak Incantations.

### Stun

When Stunned, immediately choose and discard a Challenge card from your hand.

You do not need to Recover from being Stunned. It is an instantaneous effect.

### Trip

Tripped creatures fall prone.

- Prone creatures cannot Avoid, Move, or Dash.
- Melee attacks against prone targets have favor.
- Missile attacks against prone targets have disfavor.
- Anybody engaged with a Tripped creature immediately disengages.

title: The Flow of the Crawl Phase
body:
A new game of *His Majesty the Worm* starts at the gates to the Underworld. The Crawl begins with the guild taking their first steps into those lightless depths.

The Crawl is the phase of the game centered on exploring the Underworld. When the guild leaves the safety of civilization to pursue their quests, negotiating with strange creatures, avoiding traps, and excavating ancient treasures, they are Crawling. Most of the game is focused on this phase.

The Underworld is what is called a megadungeon. A megadungeon is inherently strange. It does not need to conform to the players’ conceptions and expectations. It does benefit from having internal logic, however. Learning the “rules” of the Underworld is fun, and having internally consistent logic allows players to make meaningful choices (which is the point of the game).

A megadungeon is composed of smaller, constituent dungeons. Each dungeon is a thematic level, section, or subsection. For example, your Underworld might be composed of five dungeon levels, which are called the Spires, the Castle of Crossed Destinies, the Inverted Castle, the City of Ruin, and the Necropolis of Ot.

Dungeons are separated into different rooms. A room can be any discrete space: a chamber, a feasting hall, a natural cavern, an underground river, etc. Each room should have something interesting for the players to interact with. For example, one room in the Castle of Crossed Destinies could have a giant throne constructed of a titan’s bones, another room could be the lair of a reverse sphinx who needs to hear riddles to survive, and so on.

As the guild explores each room, random events called the Meatgrinder create tension. The Meatgrinder represents the passage of time: torches burning low, patrolling monsters, and other hazards.

**Each dungeon level has both a map and a Meatgrinder table, both full of interesting things to discover.** When players travel into a new room, the GM looks at the room description. Simultaneously, the GM draws a random card from the major arcana and consults the Meatgrinder table. They then describe the newly-entered room in the context of the random Meatgrinder encounter.

This chapter details what players need to know about the Crawl Phase. Advice for the GM for running the Crawl Phase is found. Procedures for the GM to create the Underworld are found in Appendix E: Underworld Creation see Origins of the Underworld.

Play happens through a combination of what the GM describes and the decisions the players make. It’s not a preplanned story.

During the Crawl, the players tell the GM where they want to go. They use an in-character map to make sure everybody is on the same page about where the guild currently is.

The GM describes what the guild encounters as they travel. The GM describes the contents of each room within the context of a random Meatgrinder event.

When the guild encounters dangers—crushing walls, minotaurs, anti-life fields, swindling merchants—the players and the GM engage in a dialogue.

- The GM describes the situation, the players ask questions about the scene, and the GM clarifies.

- The players describe what their adventurers attempt, the GM asks questions about their actions and their intentions, and the players clarify.

- The GM describes what happens as a result of the adventurer’s actions. If there’s risk or doubt about the adventurer’s ability to accomplish their goals, the GM calls for a test of fate.

Despite the presence of otherworldly and strange beings, very few creatures attack on sight and fight to the death. (Well, perhaps skeletons. They want to free their boney brethren from the flesh prisons that you’re cruelly keeping them in.) Almost everything can be talked to, negotiated with, or at least avoided. See “Disposition” see Social Encounters & Disposition for an overview of negotiating with the GM’s characters.

When combat cannot be avoided, the Crawl Phase transitions into the Challenge Phase. Once the Challenge is resolved, the Challenge Phase transitions back to the Crawl.

Mysteriously, the Underworld has a property that preserves life. When a mortal blow is dealt, the victim lingers at Death’s Door before dying. This property has prolonged the life of many adventurers who have run afoul of the Underworld’s treachery.

Those who *do* die in the Underworld rise again as undead…

title: Watches
body:
Travel time is measured in watches. A watch isn't a concrete period of time; it might be an hour or ten minutes or all night. It’s hard to tell how much time is *actually* passing in the Underworld. Time moves weirdly— happy moments burn away quickly, whereas tense moments stretch like lengthening shadows. Watches are just a way to talk about how quickly, quietly, and carefully you're moving.

A watch passes:

- Each time the guild passes into a new room on the map;

- Whenever the guild spends a long time performing a task (such as taking a door off of its hinges, repairing a broken machine, counting the jelly beans in a jar); and

- Each Camp and Challenge Phase.

When a watch passes, the GM draws a card from the major arcana deck and compares the result to the Meatgrinder table for that dungeon. The GM then describes the Meatgrinder event in the context of the new or current scene.

title: Meatgrinder
body:
The Meatgrinder is a collection of impermanent events—things like monster patrols, weather, or bumping into a rival guild. Importantly, the rate at which your light sources flicker and go out is controlled by the Meatgrinder.

As you travel, the GM will describe scenes that combine the contents of each room and the results of the Meatgrinder. For example, if the room you enter is a dragon’s lair and the GM randomly pulls a patrol of goblins from the Meatgrinder table, you might find goblins attempting to sneak into the dragon’s lair for treasure—and making entirely too much noise in the process.

When a Meatgrinder event is triggered, the GM should mark it off. If the cards are shuffled and the GM draws the same event twice, nothing happens and the guild has a watch of respite. The GM repopulates their Meatgrinder tables after each City Phase.

The full procedure to create a Meatgrinder for a dungeon level is detailed in Appendix E. Here are the basics.

In general, Meatgrinder tables follow this formula:

- **Torches gutter (I–V):** *Torches guttering* represents time passing as your light sources grow dim. When torches gutter, all light sources flicker.

- Torches gutter events are not marked off from the Meatgrinder. They can occur multiple times.

- **Curiosities (VI–X):** *Curiosities* are moments of serendipity. “Nothing happens” is a boring result for a random table. Curiosities are an opportunity for a GM to interject color into the black depths of the Underworld. They can serve as inspiration for role-playing exchanges or provide hints about the local environment.

- **Travel events (XI–XV):** *Travel events* represent things that go wrong during the journey. They might require you to make a hard choice about resource management or ask you to test fate to avoid a hardship. Travel events might prompt Challenges.

- **Random encounters (XVI–XX):** *Random encounters* are chance meetings with the denizens of the Underworld. These might be friendly encounters with would-be allies, hazardous encounters with hungry animals, or murderous encounters with monsters. Interesting random encounters don’t simply list “monster type.” The best random encounters paint a small scenario that the GM can use as inspiration for a Challenge.

- Q**uest rumor (XXI):** *Quest rumors* lead you one step closer to your current quest. Quest rumors provide hints or directions towards your quest goal. If you’ve already made significant progress on your current quest, this result might even feature the object of your quest.

title: Loud Noises
body: Every time the guild makes a loud noise or otherwise draws attention to themselves, the GM should pull on the Meatgrinder table. If the draw results in a random encounter (results XVI–XX), the GM describes an encounter with the wandering creature drawn to the noise. Otherwise, nothing happens.

title: Moving Carefully
body: If the guild is proceeding very slowly or methodically, tapping in front of them with their ten-foot pole, the GM should pull again on the Meatgrinder table after resolving potential encounters. If the second draw results in torches guttering (results I–V), the GM keeps the result to represent additional time passing. Otherwise, nothing happens.

title: Darkness
body:
No matter how accomplished an adventurer is, darkness is crippling. You are Blind while in darkness. Most activities are simply impossible to accomplish in the dark—others may be attempted but suffer disfavor.

Crawling in the dark is a futile pursuit. The guild is at risk of every pitfall and every trap, and creatures accustomed to darkness will find them easy prey.

title: Flickers
body:
The Omphalic Market lists candles, torches, and lanterns for purchase. Each type of light source can flicker a certain number of times before it is extinguished.

Your light source flickers when the Meatgrinder says torches gutter. Candles can flicker 2 times. Torches can flicker 3 times. Lanterns can flicker 4 times.

Candles, torches, and lanterns are not designed for violence. Your light source automatically breaks and is extinguished if:

- It is hit in combat

- You use it as a weapon in combat

You can tie your lantern to your belt, but if you suffer a Wound, your lantern is also Destroyed, covering you in oil.

Dropping your light source might cause it to go out.

- Torches have a 50% chance of staying lit or being extinguished when dropped. Extinguished torches can’t be relit.

- Candles are extinguished when dropped. Candles may be relit.

- Lanterns are Destroyed when dropped.

title: We’re Doomed!
body:
If the guild is *completely* out of light sources, each player draws on the table below.

| Card | Doom |
|:---:|---|
| I–VII | You are eaten by a grue. |
| VIII | You are captured by monsters. The GM chooses an appropriate monster based on the Meatgrinder table. Escaping guild members know your general location and the type of monster. |
| IX | You are held for ransom by the bird-faced Olu Gang. It takes 1,000 gold per guild Fame to buy your freedom. |
| X | You are lost in the Underworld. The GM places your adventurer as an encounter on the Meatgrinder table. That character may reenter play if you are encountered. Make a new adventurer for the time being. |
| Page | You make it back to the surface, but only after making solemn oaths to forsake the adventuring life. You retire. The GM controls your character in the City. |
| Knight | You scramble back to the surface naked and shivering. You lose all of your equipment, both from your belt and backpack. |
| Queen | You limp back to the surface, shaken to your core. You have a 50% chance to have lost each item carried, tested individually. |
| King | You limp back to the surface, mostly unscathed but raving about the twelve different flavors of darkness. |

title: Black Honey
body:
A thick molasses that tastes so, so sweet. It’s like meth and speed had a terrifying demon baby. You act preternaturally fast while on the honey.

Each time you take black honey, you are re-exposed to the affliction. If you’ve previously cured stage 2, erase the marking in the stage 2 section.

Stage 1: If you’ve recently taken black honey, you may choose to draw 5 cards during an encounter instead of 4. If you do so, you spit out 1–4 teeth.

Requires 5 charges to quit.

Stage 2: You’re jittery and sweaty. Your Cups score equals 1 and you suffer disfavor on all tests of fate that require fine motor control.

Requires 1 charge to calm down to stage 1.

title: Social Encounters & Disposition
body:
In the Underworld, you’ll encounter the bizarre denizens of the dungeons and the adventurers trying their best to slay them (or you).

Role-playing the characters you encounter is one of the GM’s many jobs. The GM is provided with guidelines for role-playing called Disposition. Advice for GMs creating characters with interesting personalities and running social encounters is provided in Creating interesting characters.

Each Disposition has three steps of severity: mild, basic, and intense. Mild emotions are on the inside of the circle; intense emotions are on the outside of the circle. Related emotions are grouped near each other.

title: Starting Disposition
body:
A character’s starting Disposition informs the GM how that creature should initially act, how hostile or friendly it is to the guild, and what they’re likely to demand in a parlay.

Sometimes a character’s starting Disposition will be obvious based on the scenario in which the guild encounters them. If there is any question, or if randomness is desired, the GM should look at the top card in the discard pile of the minor arcana.

| Card | Disposition |
|:---:|---|
| I–II | Anger |
| III–IV | Distaste |
| V–VI | Sadness |
| VII–VIII | Joy |
| IX–X | Surprise |
| Page–Knight | Trust |
| Queen–King | Fear |

Characters with positive Dispositions (e.g., joy, trust) negotiate in good faith. They will acquiesce to reasonable requests in exchange for a reasonable exchange from the guild. Characters with intense positive Dispositions might even provide extra aid and advice.

Characters with negative Dispositions (e.g., angry, distaste) want to bring the conversation to an end. They don’t have any interest in exchanging goods or services with the guild. If the encounter continues at all, it will be in a combative capacity—transition to the Challenge Phase.

title: Influencing Disposition
body:
As you role-play with a GM character, their Dispositions change based on how your actions interact with their personality. All intelligent creatures can be negotiated with (assuming a shared language). When role-playing with the GM’s characters, you don’t have to speak in a silly voice or use faux Shakespearean language. You don’t even have to say exactly what your character is saying, word for word. You do have to say what you want and how you are approaching the conversation.

- You might flatter the vain countess.

- You might make pious platitudes to the unctuous priest.

- You might offer rare trash to the goblin trash merchant.

These strategies only make sense when applied towards that particular character; offering trash to the vain countess would be unwise.

The GM judges the results of a negotiation based on how what you said aligns or conflicts with their character’s personality. Depending on the outcome of the role-playing exchange, the GM character’s Disposition either increases/decreases in severity or shifts to a related emotion.

- Good ideas and strong bargaining chips that appeal to a character’s likes and wants will automatically create positive Dispositions.

- Approaches that conflict with the character’s dislikes will automatically create negative Dispositions.

- When the situation is risky and there’s something at stake, the GM will call for a test of Wands.

- Success on this test moves the character’s Disposition to an adjacent, beneficial emotion or increases their current emotion’s severity.

- Failure moves the character’s Disposition towards a negative emotion.

title: The Flow of the Challenge Phase
body:
During the Crawl Phase, players and GMs exchange questions and answers. During Challenges, things become much more bounded. A Challenge narrows the lens of the adventure into every blow, every maneuver, every stunt that the players attempt.

In this phase, everybody at the table draws cards that represent their opportunities during combat. The players play these cards to choose specific actions—attacks, movement, pulling something from their pack, etc. The GM controls the hostile characters, be they monsters or mortals. Both sides pursue their goals while exchanging blows. When one side retreats, surrenders, or lies defeated, the Challenge Phase transitions back to the Crawl.

The Challenge Phase uses the following procedure. Play cycles through steps 1-5 until the Challenge is resolved. The rules for players and GMs differ slightly; GM-specific rules start see GMing the Challenge.

### 0. Set the scene

As the Challenge begins, the GM frames the scene. They describe the different zones, note the combatants and their positions, and articulate if any scenery and hazards have special rules.

### 1. Draw Challenge cards

Challenges are split up into rounds. At the beginning of each round, each player draws four Challenge cards from the minor arcana deck. The GM draws a certain number of major arcana cards based on what enemies are being fought.

### 2. Play Initiative

Players choose one of their Challenge cards as their Initiative for the round. They place it facedown in front of them. The GM plays an Initiative card for every significant character or group of characters they control.

### 3. Take turns

The GM counts up from lowest card (I) through the highest card (king). When the GM announces the Initiative of a GM character or adventurer, that character takes their turn.

### 4. Minor actions

After a turn is resolved, the GM asks: “Any minor actions?” Anybody who wants to take a minor action declares their action. Minor actions are resolved simultaneously.

After all actions of a particular Initiative number are resolved, the GM continues the count upwards. Steps 3 and 4 repeat until everybody has had their turn.

### 5. End the round

Once everybody has had their turn and the subsequent minor actions have been resolved, the round ends. Everybody discards any unused Challenge cards in their hand. Everybody discards their current Initiative card. Facedown cards remain in play. If the Fool was drawn during this past round, both minor and major arcana decks are shuffled. A new round begins.

title: Draw Challenge Cards
body:
Challenges are split up into rounds.

At the beginning of each round, each player draws four Challenge cards from the minor arcana deck. The GM draws cards from the major arcana deck based on what kind of enemies they’re using, their powers, and their numbers.

Challenge cards are spent on the following:

- Initiative

- Actions

- Minor actions

Initiative is a measure of how carefully or recklessly your adventurer is acting during that round. The lower your Initiative, the faster you take your turn. However, the lower your Initiative, the easier you are to hit during that round.

Actions are decisive deeds that determine whether you will survive or perish. The “acting player” is the player whose turn it is.

Characters take one action on their turn. Each player has one turn per round (unless they draw the Fool. The GM will take a turn for each important character they control.

Minor actions are quick actions that take place during other characters’ turns. They happen after another person’s turn and take place simultaneously to all other minor actions taken at the same time.

title: Play Initiative
body:
After drawing your Challenge cards, select one card and play it facedown. This is your Initiative for the round. The GM will play one Initiative card for each significant character or group of characters they control.

Initiative determines how quickly you act during the round. Initiative also determines your defenses.

When making a test of fate, if the total of the card’s value plus bonuses is 14+, the test is a success. The chaos of a Challenge changes this paradigm. To successfully attack, trip, cast a spell against, or otherwise affect a combatant in a Challenge, the total value of your card plus bonuses must equal or exceed that character’s Initiative.

When other characters attempt to perform an action against you, flip your Initiative card face up (if it’s not already). Compare the action’s value against your Initiative.

- If the value is greater than your Initiative, the action succeeds.

- If the value is less than your Initiative, the action fails.

- If the value ties your Initiative, it succeeds *unless* you are wielding a shield.

### Tied Initiative

If two people have the same Initiative, they act at the same time. Players with the same Initiative decide among themselves who will declare their actions first. If there is a tie between a GM character and an adventurer, the GM determines who declares first.

title: Take Turns
body:
Each round, the GM counts up from lowest card (I) through the highest card (king). When the GM reaches your Initiative in their count, flip your Initiative card face up to declare that you are the acting player.

To perform an action, play one of your Challenge cards. There are a range of actions you can perform during Challenges, each of which is tied to one of the four suits. The rules for Challenge Actions are detailed.

| Swords | Pentacles | Cups | Wands |
|---|---|---|---|
| Attack | Avoid | Aid Another | Banter |
| Riposte | Dash | Command | Speak Incantation |
| | Dodge | Pull Item from Pack | Recover |
| | Roughhouse | Use Item | |

There are also miscellaneous actions that can be performed using any card, regardless of suit.

On your turn, you can use *any* card to perform *any* action, regardless of suit.

You may only spend one card per Initiative turn. If you have performed an action, you cannot also perform a minor action. Interrupt actions do not count towards the one card per turn limit.

The GM’s characters will also take turns over the course of the round. During an enemy character’s turn, the GM plays a card from their hand and adds their character’s attribute to the total value, just like players. The GM might have several characters that take turns over the course of a round.

title: Action Value
body:
On your turn, your action’s total value equals the card’s face value *plus* the associated attribute.

For example, if an acting player makes an Attack action with a IV of Cups card and has a Swords attribute of 3, their Attack has a total value of 7 (4+3).

All Challenge Actions (both actions and minor actions) can have favor/disfavor applied, per the rules see Favor and disfavor. The GM is the arbiter of when to apply favor and disfavor during Challenges.

- Favor adds +3 to the action’s total value.

- Disfavor subtracts -3 from the action’s total value.

For example, an adventurer dangling from a tower window by a rope might be given disfavor to an Attack action against a circling harpy due to the precariousness of their situation. Alternatively, an adventurer might be given favor to a Roughhouse action against a character who is standing near a puddle of oil because the ground is so slippery.

You can spend a point of Resolve to gain favor on a Challenge Action. Resolve cannot be spent to adjust your Initiative score.

title: Facedown Cards
body:
You usually play Challenge cards face up, but some actions (such as Dodge or Aid Another) are played facedown to represent your adventurer readying themselves to act.

You may only have one facedown action at a time. (Your Initiative card does not count towards this limit!) If you take an action that would cause you to place another card facedown, replace the current facedown card and discard the last one. If you no longer want to take the facedown action, discard the card.

Facedown actions played on your turn add the appropriate attribute to the card’s value when the card is revealed. To help you remember when you played a facedown card (and to visually distinguish it from your Initiative), set the card down in a different position relative to your Initiative card.

- If you play a card facedown on your turn, set the facedown card sideways *above* your Initiative card. When the time comes to reveal the card, add the appropriate attribute to the card’s value.

- If you play a card facedown as a minor action, set the facedown card sideways *below* your Initiative card. When the time comes to reveal the card, use the face value of the card for the total.

Facedown cards remain in play until they’re activated, you replace them with another action, you discard it, or the Challenge Phase ends.

Facedown cards do not impact how many cards you draw at the beginning of the round—players always draw four Challenge cards.

You can use a defensive action (like Dodge or Riposte) for the benefit of anybody in your zone. It stays facedown until activated, as usual. If you move away from your ally before the defensive action triggers, discard your facedown card.

title: Interrupt Actions
body:
Some talents or circumstances can turn an action into an interrupt. Interrupt actions take place *before* the acting player’s action.

For example, a halfling adventurer wielding a polearm might Riposte against a goblin charging at them before the goblin's Attack is resolved. If the halfling slays the goblin, they’re safe; if the goblin survives the interrupt, they follow through on their Attack.

Interrupt actions do not count against the limit of one action per turn.

title: The Fool
body:
If you pull the Fool when drawing Challenge cards, put it in your hand as normal.

The Fool is not played like other Challenge cards. The Fool has a value of 0 and is always played in conjunction with another card. When you play the Fool, you get an additional turn—you can play any card to perform any action and add the associated attribute to your total value. Additionally, this counts as an interrupt action. The Fool *always* goes first, no matter what.

Essentially, drawing the Fool allows you to take two turns (but no minor actions) during a round.

When the Fool is drawn, shuffle both the minor and major arcana decks at the end of the round.

title: Minor Actions
body:
After the acting player’s action is resolved, the GM asks the table if anybody wants to perform a minor action. To make a minor action, spend a Challenge card whose suit *matches* the action. A Cups card must be spent to make a Cups action, a Pentacles card must be spent to make a Pentacles action, and so on.

Minor actions all happen essentially at the same time. Everybody who wants to perform a minor action declares what they want to do and puts down a card to represent this action. Then, everybody reveals their cards simultaneously. The GM describes the results of these actions in whatever order makes the most sense and is the most cinematic.

The players and the GM can both make minor actions. If the GM is performing a minor action, they declare their action at the same time as the players do. Because the GM uses the major arcana (which has no suits), they ignore the requirement that the card played must match the action’s suit—the GM can play any card to take a minor action.

After all minor actions of a particular Initiative number are resolved, the GM continues the count upwards until all characters and enemies have had a turn.

### Minor action value

A minor action’s total value equals the card’s face value. You do not add your attribute to minor actions. A minor action’s value can also be affected by favor/disfavor.

For example, to use Roughhouse as a minor action, play a Pentacles card. The card only has its face value. For instance, the Knight of Pentacles has a total value of 12— your Pentacles attribute is not added.

title: End the Round
body:
When every player and each GM character has had their turn (and all minor actions on the last turn have been resolved), the round is over. Facedown actions are left facedown. Any remaining Challenge cards in your hand are discarded. The players and the GM draw more Challenge cards and begin a new round.

If the Fool has been played, shuffle both the minor and the major arcana decks before dealing new cards.

title: GM Challenge Hand Size
body:
During step 1, draw 3 Challenge cards from the **major arcana deck,** and add:

- +1 card for each type of enemy

- +1 card if the enemies outnumber the adventurers

- +1 card if there are twice as many enemies as adventurers

- +1 card for each enemy that’s physically larger than a human

- +2 cards if there is an elite enemy

- +3 cards if there is a dungeon lord

(Elite enemies and dungeon lords are defined in Appendix C: Dungeon Denizens.)

Each of these additional cards is cumulative. Check to see which are applicable at the start of each round. Therefore, if the number of enemies decreases, you will draw fewer major arcana cards.

For example, if there are twelve imps facing four adventurers, the GM draws 6 cards from the major arcana deck. That includes: 3 cards to start, +1 for the one type of enemy (imps), +1 for having more enemies than adventurers, +1 for having twice as many enemies than adventurers.

If, over the course of the Challenge, the adventurers reduce the number of imps to seven, the GM will draw 5 cards at the beginning of the next round: 3 + 1 for having one enemy type (imps) and +1 for having more enemies than adventurers.

title: Enemy Actions
body:
When the Initiative count reaches the number of an enemy’s Initiative, flip it face up and take a turn for that character.

Play a Challenge card from your hand to take an action.

The number of the major arcana is the card’s value (e.g., Temperance [XIV] has a value of 14). Because the major arcana spans the values 1–21, they are categorized into two types of *doom*: lesser dooms and greater dooms. These speak to the sorts of dangers they involve for the adventurers.

Major arcana from I-XIV are lesser dooms.

Major arcana from XV-XXI are greater dooms.

Appendix C: Dungeon Denizens has examples of lesser and greater dooms for each creature.

title: Lesser Dooms
body:
Lesser doom cards have values of 1–14. These are analogous to the minor arcana (which are also rated from 1–14).

Play any lesser doom card to make any Challenge Action (Attack, Dash, Dodge, etc.). Only add the enemy’s attributes to the action’s value on their turn, as normal.

You also play a lesser doom card to use an enemy’s lesser doom abilities. These are special powers or tactics unique to that creature.

title: Greater Dooms
body:
Greater doom cards have values of 15–21.

Play a greater doom to activate an enemy’s greater doom abilities. These represent a creature’s most potent powers.

- If the greater doom only affects the environment or yourself, the greater doom occurs as soon as you play the card.

- If the greater doom targets an adventurer, your greater doom is played at the same time as a lesser doom card used as an Attack action. If the Attack is successful, the greater doom effect occurs.

- The greater doom card is played regardless of whether the Attack hits or not.

For example, a dragon has a greater doom called “Breath of Fire.” To use it on an adventurer, use a lesser doom card (like the Hanged Man [XII]) to make an Attack action. At the same time, play any greater doom card (like the Star [XVII]) to activate Breath of Fire. If the Attack is successful, the adventurer is roasted by the dragon’s fiery breath and takes that effect.

You cannot typically play a greater doom card to make Challenge Actions like Attack, Dash, Dodge, etc. You *can* play greater doom cards to use any miscellaneous action except for Vigilance.

You can discard a greater doom to gain favor on another Challenge Action.

Lastly, some opponents have the ability to use greater doom cards for Challenge Actions. This represents a creature’s overwhelming, extra-human capabilities. For example, a giant may be able to use a greater doom like the Tower [XVI] as an Attack with a value of 16.

title: Patrol
body:
Keep guard, patrolling around your encampment and setting up assault counter-measures.

If an adventurer takes the Patrol action, the GM draws twice on the Meatgrinder table and takes a non-random encounter result if available during step 3 of the Camp Phase (No Rest for the Wicked).

title: No Rest for the Wicked
body:
While the adventurers rest, the Underworld continues to go about its sinister business. The GM should draw on the Meatgrinder table for the dungeon the adventurers are camping in. Random events like rainstorms and rising fog will add flavor to the Camp Phase, but probably won’t impose any special rules. If a random encounter is pulled, the Camp Phase is interrupted by a Challenge.

It is assumed that adventurers are taking turns guarding the camp while they rest. If a random encounter occurs, the GM should randomly determine who’s on watch. An easy way to do this is to look at the top card of the minor arcana discard pile: the suit displayed corresponds to the path of the adventurer on guard. If there are multiple adventurers of the same path, odd cards target the player to the GM’s left, even cards target the player to the GM’s right.

The adventurer on guard tests Cups.

- If successful, the alarm is raised before the random encounter finds the encampment. The guild has a brief period of time to prepare.

- If the test of fate is a failure, the guild is surprised.

- A guild caught asleep is very vulnerable—nobody is wearing armor or actively wielding weapons.

- See Special rules for the rules about surprise.

If one of the players has performed the Patrol action, the GM draws twice on the Meatgrinder table and chooses a non-encounter result if possible. A Challenge only occurs if both cards reveal a random encounter—the GM chooses which one to use.

Once the Challenge is resolved, the Camp Phase continues on to step 4.

title: Overland Travel
body:
Most of *His Majesty the Worm* is focused on dungeon-crawling in the mythic Underworld. It abstracts concerns of food, time, and distance into the flow of the Camp and Crawl Phases. If your game takes place in a setting where the sun shines, you might want a system that adds an additional layer of logistics to travel. If this is the case, use these rules.

Each day is divided into three watches. Each watch takes about eight hours.

Using these optional rules, timekeeping becomes important. After three watches, a new day dawns. The GM should step through the calendar as they pass day by day.

For each watch, follow this procedure:

### 1. Choose action

The guild chooses a single overland action to take.

### 2. Meatgrinder

The GM draws on their Meatgrinder table for a random event or encounter.

### 3. Set the scene

The GM describes the random encounter in the context of the hex the guild is traveling in. The players and the GM play out the scene, and when it’s over, the GM calls for a new overland action to be chosen.

title: City Events
body:
The City is never dull. When you stagger back to the surface, you enter a chaotic metropolis with all its intrigues, politics, and spectacle.

Before play, the GM should construct a City Events table, detailing potential events that frame the guild’s visit. During this step, the GM draws a major arcana card, consults this table, and interprets the result.

The results need not happen instantly. For example, if the GM draws a rumor on the City Events table, they might have a contract-giver or shopkeeper deliver the rumor to the guild through the course of playing out the City Phase.

The City Events table should have twenty-one entries and follow this formula:

Curiosities (I–V): *Curiosities* are moments of serendipity. GMs, use these events to inject color into the game. Curiosities create a sense of realness, and are opportunities to demonstrate what kind of City exists in your game. Are curiosity events dark portents like raving madmen calling for repentance under the blood moon or are they bucolic affairs with Punch and Judy puppet shows?

Happenings (VI–X): *Happenings* are events that frame the lives of the residents of the City. These are the saturnalia, feasts, and holy days that break up the wheel of years. Happenings should make the City Phase feel *different* to the players.

Visiting the City during a religious festival is much different than visiting during the normal drudge of days.

Rumors (XI–XV): *Rumors* hint at the contents of the Underworld: treasures, monsters, traps, hazards, and history. Rumors are opportunities to tell lore and define the “story” of the dungeon.

Travel events (XVI–XX): *Travel events* are often things that go wrong in a trip to the City, or a chance to confront the players to make a hard choice.

Signs and Portents (XXI): *Signs and portents* are special events wherein weirdness has spilled out from the Underworld and complicates life in the City. When drawing this result, the GM looks at the top card of the minor arcana discard pile and then consults the Signs and Portents table.

Like the Meatgrinder table, the GM should keep the City Events table tidy and fresh, swapping out used entries with new content (see step 8).

The next page has an example City Events table to serve as a starting point.

title: Signs and Portents
body:
| Card | Signs and Portents |
|:---:|---|
| I | The Mushroom Forest: Yellow mushrooms with pink gills erupt from the flagstones of a random district. They stand as tall as columns, choking off traffic and commerce. That district’s special City Action cannot be used this City Phase. |
| II | The Blood-Red Star: A comet hangs over the City: a menstrual smear across the sky in both night and day. Its sickly red-brown light causes sleeplessness in the City. Everyone you meet is haggard and bleary eyed, barely holding onto sanity. You can’t sleep either. Begin the next Crawl Phase Stressed. |
| III | Mists: Emerald-colored fog rises out of the mouths to the Underworld, blotting out the sun. This phase takes place in an unnatural, unending night. |
| IV | The Ancient Quarter: A district of the City that hasn’t existed in centuries reappears from the mists of the ages. Its residents are antique and deeply confused about where they are and how they got here. Draw an additional district, per the rules in Appendix D: City Creation. This district becomes a part of the present City. |
| V | The Honey Cult: An adventurer brought black honey to the City. All who eat of it profess to see terrible and wonderful visions of a pregnant woman. They rave about the Nascent Horde. Something about their ravings causes a deep anxiety inside of you. |
| VI | Dreams: Dreams begin wandering through the City. A husband begins having the dreams of his wife. A hay merchant suddenly begins having the dreams of his neighbor, a clothier. The archbishop begins having dreams of the inbred princess locked in the Tower of Forbiddance. Each player must tell one secret to another player, which their adventurer dreams about. |
| VII | The Slime Catastrophe: Some thoughtless adventurer brought a baby green slime back into the City. It quickly grew in size, swallowing the food of the inn, the horses, the stables, and entire city blocks. Before it was vanquished with a fire, it ate an entire district. Remove one random district from the City. |
| VIII | Rip Van Winkle: Time moves strangely in the Underworld. You return to the City a full 100 years after you left. Your children are old and your lovers are dead. |
| IX | The Thing in the Water: Fishermen pull forth the corpse of some stupendous white thing from the waters of the Grey. The ocean sings a pitiful song for the next week. The song elicits a feeling of devastating ennui. Several named GM characters walk into the ocean and are never seen again. |
| X | Dog Suicide: All the dogs of the City commit suicide overnight: they throw themselves off of bridges, smash themselves through glass windows, drown themselves in the Grey. |
| XI | The Black Miracle of the Statues: Every graven image in the City begins speaking terrible blasphemies. In a fit of iconoclasm, the Cult begins smashing all representative art. Statues, paintings, idols, mosaics, figureheads, children’s toys, etc., are all fuel for their holy bonfires. Religious fervor is increased in the near term. |
| XII | The Blood Rain: Red clouds, swollen like pustules, begin raining blood. The blood flows in rivulets down the streets, pollutes cisterns, stains everything. It’s gross. |
| XIII | The Child of Silence: A giant infant crawls down the streets. The thing is surrounded with an aura of impenetrable silence: no sound whatsoever carries in its presence. It seems to be hunting the guild. The guild can hide and forestall this doom, though the Child of Silence will doubtless cause some terrible harm if left unchecked. |
| XIV | What Is Dead May Never Die: The dead haunt the City. Until the guild finds the source of the undead in the Underworld and quells it, no City Actions can be performed. If the plague of the dead is allowed to continue, the Underworld will breach onto the surface, devouring the City (as it has devoured so many cities before). |

Refer to this table when the City Events table yields a XXI result.

title: Beg and Busk
body:
Sing for coin in streets and winesinks.

- Draw from the minor arcana deck. You gain gold equal to the numerical value of your draw + Wands attribute.

title: Carouse
body:
Spend everything you earned on drinking and whoring.

- If you spend 50% of the gold you brought back, you get 1XP.

- If you spend 100% of the gold you brought back, you get 2XP.

- In addition, the GM draws a major arcana and consults the Hangover table on the next page to see if there are any complications later.

Carousing is an easy, micro-math way to get a meaningful benefit. It emulates genre staples of heroes like Conan or Fafhrd and the Gray Mouser frittering away last adventure’s booty and starting each story dead broke.

Anything in brackets [ ] refers to the top card of the minor arcana discard pile. Sometimes brackets provide numerical values—for example, “[value] in gold coins” provides a number of gold coins equal to the value of the card. Sometimes brackets provide a random result within the table’s entry based on the suit of the card.

| Card | Hangover |
|:---: | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| I | You awaken with a headache and [value] new gold coins in your pocket. |
| II | You awaken in the stocks, having been left there to sober up. A little bruised, but no worse for wear. Your guild has a chance to make fun of you and throw tomatoes before you’re let out. |
| III | A cowbell has been hung around your neck by a steel chain. The chain is too small to remove over your head, and you don’t have the key to the lock. You clang while you walk until you solve this problem. |
| IV | You awaken with a marriage ring on your finger, but no memory of who you married. |
| V | You awaken in a strange room, heavy pounding on the door. The people on the other side are very angrily demanding their money back. |
| VI | You awaken with a marriage ring on a finger. The finger isn’t yours. |
| VII | You awaken next to a [even] High Priest / [odd] High Priestess. They thank you for helping them break their vows, and showing them what it means to be happy. |
| VIII | You are thrown through the window of your usual watering hole. “And stay out!” the proprietor adds. You are banned from your favorite tavern. |
| IX | You awaken with a new tattoo. • The tattoo is on your: [Swords] arm, [Pentacles] leg, [Cups] backside, [Wands] face.• It is: [1–2] horribly offensive, [3–8] cringe and misspelled, [9–King] pretty sweet. |
| X | You awaken in an alley. Hobos around you seem to be wearing your clothes and armor. But you’re wearing theirs, so… even trade? |
| XI | A letter is delivered to your room. It’s from a local aristocrat and they have accepted your invitation to a duel. The duel is in [value] days. |
| XII | You awaken in the muck of a cistern wearing only your smallclothes. All the other items you had on you are missing as well. |
| XIII | You don’t remember exactly what you did, but by the wanted posters lining the streets you can guess it wasn’t good. Choose based on the card’s suit: [Swords] armed robbery, [Pentacles] attempted pick-pocketing, [Cups] lewd acts, [Wands] consorting with dark entities. |
| XIV | You find an invoice in your pouch: “Sold: One (1) soul for one (1) beer.” Your signature and an infernal symbol seal the contract. |
| XV | You awaken to the sounds of sobbing. There seems to be a nobleman hogtied and gagged on your floor. How strange. |
| XVI | Why are you naked and wearing a fake bull’s head? Why does this shop have nothing but broken plates and smashed glass? Why are the shopkeeper and constables so angry and yelling? |
| XVII | Luckily, it only burns when you pass water. |
| XVIII | You back your way out of a blackout wrapped in a warm snuggly robe standing in a candle lit room. It’s nice. “The dark pact is complete,” a deep baritone voice says, “Go forth, my brothers, and see that our lord’s will be done.”... Wait, what? |
| XIX | Where the hells is your hand? Why can you feel it crawling? |
| XX | You remember agreeing to a dare. It had something to do with a candle and flatulence. • You find that: [Swords] a district is burnt down[Pentacles] two districts are burnt down[Cups] three districts are burnt down[Wands] as Cups, but a fire spirit is still roaming the City, and the sorcerers are pissed. |
| XXI | Did you always have those circular puncture marks on your neck? I’m sure it’ll be fiiiine. |

title: Leeches
body: Leeches draw out bad blood and let good yellow phlegm and bile reign in your poisoned body. You can apply leeches to another adventurer with the Use an Item Camp Action. If you do so, draw a card. If you draw a Swords or Pentacles card, nothing happens. If you draw a Cups or Wands card, the leeched adventurer counts as having burned 2 charges to cure an affliction.

title: Area Sense
body:
Spend 2 Resolve to meditate for a watch. Draw a card. The GM describes a number of interesting “visions” equal to the value of the drawn card during your meditation. (Remember, pages have a value of 11, knights have a value of 12, etc.)

These visions are sensual experiences. For example, you could hear the clatter of dice as bored crusaders gamble, smell a nearby goblin taking a shit, hear two guards talking about who’s going to be on duty tonight, sense the quivering tension of a spike trap embedded in the ceiling, and so on.

The GM provides these details by looking at their map and Meatgrinder table. Starting near the player’s current area and moving outwards, the GM provides one salient detail from the nearby rooms. Each vision should yield a meaningful detail (e.g., the presence of a hidden door, detected by an imperceptible draft in a nearby room), but need not be elaborate or detailed (i.e., the GM need not mention that the door is trapped, or that it is activated by pulling on a statue’s arm).

Any Meatgrinder events that occur when a watch passes are resolved after the GM has given your visions.

title: Counsel
body:
Any time during a Challenge, you may yell advice to another adventurer and hand the player a card from your hand. The card’s suit must match the action you counsel them to take. The other player may play that card as long as they use it to take your advice. In all other ways, it is treated as a normal Challenge card.

If you spend a Resolve when you use this talent, the other player may play this card as an interrupt. Otherwise, the standard one-card-per-turn rule remains in effect.

You may use this talent once per round.

title: High Chant
body: This talent gives you a new Camp Action: Perform the High Chant. When you Perform the High Chant, speak at least two stanzas of epic poetry for the table. If you do so, you may select a number of cards from the minor arcana discard pile equal to your Cups. These are called inspiration cards. Distribute the cards to other players— you may keep one for yourself. No player can ever have more than one inspiration card. Inspiration cards may be spent as actions during a Challenge. Alternatively, you may spend your inspiration card instead of drawing from the deck when you test fate or push fate. Inspiration cards last until used or until the end of the session, whichever comes first.

title: Creating Surprises
body:
If things are beginning to get stagnant, introduce something that changes the Challenge dramatically. Let the potential energy transfer into kinetic energy.

Here’s a table to inspire ideas for different ways to shake things up. Glance at the top of the minor arcana discard pile for a random event:

| Card | Twist |
|:---:|---|
| I | The current conflict smashes down a nearby wall, connecting the current room to an adjacent one. What dwells in that room, and how will it interact with the current scene? |
| II | A wandering monster wanders into the scene. |
| III | An infantile wandering monster wanders into the scene. It imprints onto a random adventurer. |
| IV | A rival adventuring party, currently struggling with problems of their own, crash onto the scene. |
| V | Something in the current scene is revealed to only be an illusion. |
| VI | Something in the scene is revealed, with further investigation, to be something completely different. The orc is just three goblins in a trench coat. The gargoyle is just a statue. |
| VII | A secret passage is revealed, leading to hidden treasure or danger. |
| VIII | A chance blow rips open a backpack, causing two alchemical potions to crash onto the ground. As the two reagents mix, a terrible ooze begins to form, farting out magical clouds as it does. |
| IX | Disembodied spirits climb inside the bodies of nearby slain foes and reanimate them as flesh puppets. The spirits have their own agendas. What do they want? |
| X | A dropped torch or knocked over brazier starts a fire which quickly becomes a hazard as the area is filled with smoke and flames. |
| Page | The area becomes suddenly flooded with water/wine/sludge/something else. What is the source of this deluge? |
| Knight | The next time an adventurer is dealt a Wound, they are dazed. When they come to their senses, they distinctly remember a preternatural voice speaking to them. What is this divine revelation? Is it real? Or is there something going wrong in the unknowable space behind their eyes that’s undiagnosed? |
| Queen | Death shows up and stands quietly in the corner. He’s a few minutes early, but is willing to wait. |
| King | A stampede of doppelgangers barrels down the passage and accidentally bumps into the guild. They get all tangled up, tripping over each other. After the confusion, the party is substantially larger. Each player has at least two, but maybe as many as four new adventurers. Not even the players are sure who is who. Give them new adventurer sheets for their clones until things are sorted out. |

title: Augury
body:
### Component:

*A copy of the Codex Sophia, the sacred book of the Mythric cult*

The sorcerer receives supernatural wisdom to intuit the meaning of signs and portents by practicing rhapsodomancy—a form of book-based divination. This allows them to avoid the consequences of failure or great failures by looking into the future and forestalling doom.

If a test of fate would be called for and the sorcerer has time to consult a random page of the Codex Sophia and cast this spell, the GM draws a card for the test of fate instead of the player. Without showing it to the players, the GM describes the contents of the card as a parable from the Codex Sophia.

- For example, the X of Swords might yield the description, “And it said that / a man, stabbed many times, lying face down / may be very wounded, or very dead. / His face is turned away.”

- The sorcerer’s player uses their out-of-character knowledge of the iconography of the tarot to interpret these results in character. They are not allowed to use outside help (no checking your phone).

- Once the spell has been cast, a member of the guild may choose to attempt the task or not. If the attempt is made, the GM reveals the card.

- Because casting this spell technically happens before making the test of fate, the player who makes the attempt of the test may elect to spend a Resolve for favor. They may also push fate with this test as normal.

- If the guild decides not to take a course of action based on the augury, the bound by fate rule is still obeyed. The card is discarded. That action would either be successful (or not) until the situation significantly changes.

title: Brainfever
body:
### Component:

*A pouch of powder made from wild marjoram, thyme, verbena, and myrtle leaves*

The sorcerer takes a pinch of the component powder and blows it towards the target of this spell. The target enters a rage.

- The target gains favor on Attacks, but they must always play the lowest-value card they draw for their Initiative.

- Creatures that don’t feel emotions (such as nonsentient constructs) are not affected by this spell.

This spell is maintained with concentration.

title: Maleficence
body:
When magic goes wrong, draw on the appropriate maleficence table. This represents a catastrophic intrusion of the energies of the far realms into the realm of Flesh.

Here are some circumstances where magic goes wrong:

- A sorcerer is hurt while concentrating on a spell

- Two spells cancel each other out

- A sorcerer is interrupted during spellcasting

- Other instances, per the GM's devising

When maleficence comes into play, things get interesting.

### Maleficence of the Wastes

| Card | Result |
|:---:|---|
| I | A number of imps appear equal to the value of the top card in the minor arcana’s discard pile. |
| II | The sorcerer vomits a pregnant rat, which falls to the ground and explodes as a surfeit of baby rats spill bloodily from her belly. Disease oozes from the undulating mass. |
| III | The putrid stink of a mass grave fills the area. If the adventurers stay in the area, they all become Stressed due to the smell. |
| IV | A chill, green fog rises. All exposed metal items the guild carries suffer two Notches as the fog rusts and corrupts the metal. |
| V | Demonic flies fill this room. All perishable items will soon be consumed as the flies swarm into their packs, leaving behind demonic maggots. |
| VI | Wounds stop closing. The guild cannot heal Wounded talents or the Injured condition until the Fool causes the decks to be reshuffled. |
| VII | The sorcerer begins to uncontrollably weep blood. It’s hard to see anything through bloody tears (disfavor in combat or any vision-based activities). And, of course, it ruins their robes. This persists until the Fool causes the decks to be reshuffled. |
| VIII | An invisible mark appears on the sorcerer’s forehead. The sorcerer becomes the primary target of all undead creatures. They wish to talk to them, to touch them, to kiss them, to hurt them, to make them understand. This lasts until the mark is cleansed (per GM’s discretion). |
| IX | The sorcerer’s shadow departs to perform some mischief. It will return once it’s gotten into some sort of trouble. Until then, the sorcerer lacks a shadow. |
| X | The sorcerer immediately begins vomiting up a parasitic twin in chunks: little hands, eyes, hair, teeth. They can do very little until the whole thing is out. In a Challenge, this gives the sorcerer the Stunned effect. This maleficence lasts for an entire watch. |
